questions.stem: Which of the following is OUTSIDE the scope of AS 12?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — scope of AS 12

options.text: Cash subsidies conditional on future compliance
options.explanation: Cash assistance for past or future compliance with conditions is the core of the definition.

options.text: Government participation in the ownership of the enterprise
options.explanation: Equity participation by government is expressly left out of AS 12, along with changing-price problems and non-grant assistance.

options.text: Land allotted by the State at a concessional rate
options.explanation: Non-monetary grants at concessional rates are dealt with by the standard.

options.text: Duty drawbacks received against export obligations
options.explanation: Duty drawbacks are named in the standard as a form of government grant.

questions.stem: Which government assistance FAILS the AS 12 definition of a government grant?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — definition of government grants

options.text: A duty drawback on exports
options.explanation: Duty drawbacks are listed among government grants.

options.text: A cash incentive for setting up in a notified area
options.explanation: Cash assistance against conditions is squarely a grant.

options.text: Land given free of cost for building a school
options.explanation: A non-monetary grant — inside the definition, recorded at nominal value.

options.text: Free technical advice whose value cannot reasonably be measured
options.explanation: Assistance that cannot reasonably have a value placed on it is excluded from the definition.

questions.stem: An enterprise has RECEIVED a grant in cash, but it is doubtful whether it can meet the conditions attached. Under AS 12 the enterprise should:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — recognition conditions

options.text: Credit the grant directly to capital reserve
options.explanation: Capital reserve is for promoters'-contribution grants, not for parking doubtful compliance.

options.text: Not recognise the grant until there is reasonable assurance that the conditions will be complied with
options.explanation: Recognition needs BOTH assurances — compliance and receipt; receipt by itself is not conclusive evidence.

options.text: Recognise half the grant on a prudent basis
options.explanation: AS 12 knows no partial-prudence recognition — the twin-assurance test is all or nothing.

options.text: Recognise the grant — receipt is conclusive
options.explanation: The standard says receipt is NOT of itself conclusive evidence that conditions will be fulfilled.

questions.stem: A central investment subsidy is given with reference to the total capital outlay of an undertaking, with no repayment ordinarily expected. It is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — promoters' contribution

options.text: Credited to the P&L as other income in the year of receipt
options.explanation: It is not earned income — recognising it in the P&L is exactly what the standard forbids.

options.text: Credited to general reserve, available for dividend
options.explanation: The credit is capital reserve, and dividend distribution is expressly barred.

options.text: Treated as deferred income over a reasonable period
options.explanation: Deferred income is the income-approach route for asset grants — not for promoters' contribution.

options.text: Credited to capital reserve — neither distributable as dividend nor treated as deferred income
options.explanation: Promoters'-contribution grants follow the capital approach: shareholders' funds, locked both ways.

questions.stem: A State authority gives an enterprise land: plot A at a concessional price of ₹40 lakh (market value ₹15 crore) and plot B entirely free of cost. AS 12 records:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — non-monetary government grants

options.text: Both plots at market value, with the difference as deferred income
options.explanation: AS 12 has no fair-value grossing-up for non-monetary grants.

options.text: Plot A at ₹40 lakh; plot B left unrecorded since nothing was paid
options.explanation: Leaving a free grant out of the books entirely violates the standard — a nominal value is required.

options.text: Both plots at nominal values
options.explanation: Only the free asset takes a nominal value; the concessional one is recorded at its acquisition cost.

options.text: Plot A at ₹40 lakh (acquisition cost); plot B at a nominal value
options.explanation: Concessional-rate assets go in at acquisition cost; free assets at a nominal value — never at market value, never at nil-and-unrecorded.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Asset grant — deduction method
questions.stem: A machine costs ₹60,00,000 (useful life 5 years, salvage ₹8,00,000). A government grant of ₹12,00,000 is received and deducted from the asset's cost. Annual SLM depreciation is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — grants related to specific fixed assets, Method I

options.explanation: That is simply the grant amount, not a depreciation figure.
That ignores the grant — under the deduction method the grant shrinks the asset's cost.
That forgets the salvage value of ₹8,00,000.
Depreciable base = 60,00,000 − 12,00,000 − 8,00,000 salvage, spread over 5 years.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Asset grant — deferred income method
questions.stem: Same machine — cost ₹60,00,000, life 5 years, salvage ₹8,00,000, grant ₹12,00,000 — but the grant is treated as deferred income. Each year the P&L shows:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — grants related to specific fixed assets, Method II

options.text: Depreciation ₹10,40,000 and no grant income
options.explanation: The deferred grant must be released to the P&L systematically over the life.

options.text: Depreciation ₹10,40,000 and grant income ₹2,40,000
options.explanation: The asset stays at full cost — depreciation (60 − 8)/5 — while the grant releases 12,00,000/5 a year.

options.text: Depreciation ₹12,00,000 and grant income ₹2,40,000
options.explanation: The depreciation base is cost less salvage — 52,00,000 over 5 years, not 60,00,000.

options.text: Depreciation ₹8,00,000 and no grant income
options.explanation: That is the deduction method — under deferred income the asset keeps its gross cost.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Grant covering the whole cost
questions.stem: Under the deduction method, where a grant equals the whole (or virtually the whole) cost of the asset, the asset is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — nominal value rule

options.text: Excluded from the balance sheet entirely
options.explanation: An asset the enterprise owns and uses cannot vanish — the nominal value keeps it on record.

options.text: Shown in the balance sheet at a nominal value
options.explanation: The asset stays visible at a token figure rather than disappearing from the balance sheet.

options.text: Shown at full cost with the grant as a liability forever
options.explanation: A permanent liability contradicts both presentation methods.

options.text: Revalued to market value with a corresponding reserve
options.explanation: AS 12 never routes grants through revaluation.

questions.stem: A grant that compensates a running expense (say, salaries) may be presented:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — presentation of grants related to revenue

options.text: Only under 'Other Income'
options.explanation: Netting against the related expense is equally permitted.

options.text: Only as a deduction from the related expense
options.explanation: A separate credit — including under Other Income — is equally permitted.

options.text: Only directly in capital reserve
options.explanation: Capital reserve is for promoters'-contribution grants, not revenue support.

options.text: Either as a separate credit / 'Other Income' in the P&L, or as a deduction from the related expense
options.explanation: Both presentations are acceptable alternatives for revenue grants.

questions.stem: An enterprise receives a lump sum of ₹1,20,00,000 to support salary costs of ₹45,00,000 a year for the next four years. Grant income recognised each year is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — revenue grant spread over periods

options.explanation: No basis — the grant runs for four years, not eight.
Immediate recognition mismatches the four years of costs the grant supports.
That is the salary bill, not the grant — the grant covers only part of it each year.
The lump sum spreads straight-line over the four years it compensates: 1,20,00,000 ÷ 4.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Compensation for past costs
questions.stem: A grant becomes receivable as compensation for losses already incurred in a PREVIOUS accounting period, with no further related costs. It is recognised:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — grants compensating past expenses/losses

options.text: In the P&L of the period in which it becomes receivable — as an extraordinary item if appropriate under AS 5
options.explanation: There is nothing left to match — the income lands when receivable, flagged extraordinary where AS 5 warrants.

options.text: By restating the P&L of the period in which the losses were incurred
options.explanation: Prior periods are not reopened for grants that become receivable later.

options.text: As deferred income over the following five years
options.explanation: Deferral needs future related costs — here there are none.

options.text: Directly in capital reserve
options.explanation: This is income-approach territory, not promoters' contribution.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Refund of a revenue grant
questions.stem: A revenue grant becomes refundable to the extent of ₹24,00,000. The unamortised deferred credit for that grant stands at ₹10,00,000. The P&L is debited with:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — refund of revenue grants

options.explanation: The refund goes first against the ₹10,00,000 deferred credit; only the excess hits the P&L.
That is the deferred credit itself — the P&L takes the EXCESS over it.

options.text: Nil — refunds adjust reserves
options.explanation: Only promoters'-contribution refunds touch (capital) reserve; revenue-grant refunds run through deferred credit then P&L.
That ignores the deferred credit that must absorb the refund first.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Refund of an asset grant
questions.stem: A company refunds a grant that had been deducted from a machine's cost, adds the refund to the asset's book value — and then reworks depreciation of all past years retrospectively. Per AS 12:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — refund of asset grants, prospective depreciation

options.text: Both steps are correct
options.explanation: The book-value increase is right; the retrospective depreciation adjustment is expressly improper.

options.text: Increasing the book value is correct, but depreciation must be recomputed PROSPECTIVELY over the residual life — the retrospective rework violates the standard
options.explanation: The revised book value is depreciated over the remaining life only; closed years stay closed.

options.text: Both steps are wrong — the refund must go to the P&L at once
options.explanation: For asset grants the refund adjusts the asset (or deferred income), not the P&L directly.

options.text: The refund should instead reduce capital reserve
options.explanation: Capital reserve absorbs refunds of promoters'-contribution grants, not asset grants.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Refund with WDV depreciation
questions.stem: Machinery costing ₹1,000 lakh carried a grant of ₹200 lakh credited to its cost; depreciation is 20% WDV. After exactly two years' depreciation, the grant becomes fully refundable. The revised book value on refund is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — refund recorded by increasing book value

options.text: ₹512 lakh
options.explanation: That is the book value BEFORE adding back the refunded grant.

options.text: ₹800 lakh
options.explanation: That is the original net cost — two years of depreciation have passed.

options.text: ₹712 lakh
options.explanation: Net cost 800 → 640 → 512 after two years at 20% WDV; add back the ₹200 lakh refund.

options.text: ₹840 lakh
options.explanation: That takes only one year's depreciation before the refund.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Refund of promoters' contribution
questions.stem: Where a grant in the nature of promoters' contribution becomes refundable on non-fulfilment of conditions, the amount recoverable by the government is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — refund of promoters' contribution

options.text: Reduced from the capital reserve
options.explanation: The refund unwinds the original credit — capital reserve comes down.

options.text: Charged to the P&L over five years
options.explanation: Neither immediate nor spread P&L charges apply — the original entry never touched income.

options.text: Adjusted against securities premium
options.explanation: Securities premium has statutory uses only — grant refunds are not among them.

options.text: Deducted from the related asset's book value
options.explanation: Promoters'-contribution grants attach to total investment, not to a specific asset.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Character of a refund
questions.stem: Under AS 12 read with AS 5, a government grant that becomes refundable is treated as:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — refund as extraordinary item

options.text: A prior period item
options.explanation: Nothing was wrong with the earlier accounting — the refund is a current-period event.

options.text: An extraordinary item
options.explanation: The standard expressly routes refunds through extraordinary-item treatment under AS 5.

options.text: An ordinary operating expense
options.explanation: The refund's size and incidence take it outside ordinary activities — AS 12 labels it extraordinary.

options.text: A change in accounting policy
options.explanation: No policy changed; a condition failed.

questions.stem: AS 12 requires disclosure of:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — disclosure requirements

options.text: The accounting policy for grants (including presentation methods) and the nature and extent of grants recognised — including non-monetary grants at concession or free of cost
options.explanation: Two limbs: the policy adopted, and the nature/extent of recognised grants with non-monetary grants called out.

options.text: The names of the sanctioning authorities and dates of application
options.explanation: AS 12 asks for accounting substance, not administrative history.

options.text: Nothing, once the grant is fully credited to income
options.explanation: Disclosure applies to the policy and the grants recognised, however presented.

options.text: Only the total cash received from government during the year
options.explanation: Cash received is neither limb; policy and nature/extent are what the standard demands.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Grants tied to a purpose
questions.stem: A company received ₹8 crore for setting up a factory in a backward area and used ₹2 crore of it to pay dividend. It also received land free of cost and left it unrecorded. Per AS 12:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — utilisation and non-monetary recording

options.text: Both treatments are acceptable
options.explanation: Each treatment breaches a separate rule of the standard.

options.text: Only the unrecorded land is wrong; dividend from a grant is a management call
options.explanation: A grant received for a specific purpose must be utilised for that purpose alone.

options.text: Both treatments are wrong — a specific-purpose grant cannot fund dividend, and the free land must be recorded at a nominal value
options.explanation: Grants must be applied to their prescribed purpose, and free non-monetary grants enter the books at nominal value.

options.text: Only the dividend use is wrong; unrecorded land is fine since nothing was paid
options.explanation: Free assets are recorded at a nominal value — silence in the books is a violation.

questions.stem: AS 14 does NOT deal with:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U2 — scope, acquisitions excluded

readLink.label: Read: AS 14 — the standard at a glance →

options.text: The treatment of goodwill arising on amalgamation
options.explanation: Goodwill on amalgamation is expressly covered, with the five-year amortisation guidance.

options.text: Amalgamations in the nature of merger
options.explanation: Mergers are half the standard — pooling of interests territory.

options.text: Amalgamations in the nature of purchase
options.explanation: Purchases are the other half — purchase-method territory.

options.text: Acquisitions in which the acquired company is not dissolved and continues as a separate entity
options.explanation: Continued separate existence is the distinguishing feature that takes an acquisition outside AS 14.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 14 · After the balance sheet date
questions.stem: An amalgamation takes effect after the year's balance sheet date, before either company's financial statements are issued. In those statements the amalgamation is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U2 — amalgamation after the balance sheet date

readLink.label: Read: AS 14 — the standard at a glance →

options.text: Disclosed in accordance with AS 4, but not incorporated — though it may, for instance, support the going concern assumption
options.explanation: Post-year-end schemes are AS 4 disclosure events; the year's numbers stay un-amalgamated.

options.text: Ignored entirely until the next annual report
options.explanation: AS 4 disclosure is required; silence is not an option.

options.text: Incorporated, but only under the pooling of interests method
options.explanation: The method question never arises — nothing is incorporated this year.

options.text: Incorporated retrospectively from the start of the year
options.explanation: The amalgamation belongs to the next period's accounting — only disclosure this year.

questions.stem: A scheme provides an additional payment to the transferor's shareholders contingent on future profits. At the date of amalgamation the payment is NOT reasonably estimable. AS 14 requires the adjustment to be:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U2 — consideration adjustments for future events

readLink.label: Read: AS 14 — the standard at a glance →

options.text: Charged directly to capital reserve when paid
options.explanation: The adjustment flows through consideration, not straight to reserves.

options.text: Recognised as soon as the amount is determinable
options.explanation: Probable AND reasonably estimable at the date → include in consideration; otherwise recognise when determinable.

options.text: Included in consideration at the date of amalgamation on a best-guess basis
options.explanation: Inclusion at the amalgamation date needs the payment to be probable and reasonably estimable.

options.text: Ignored — consideration is fixed at the date of amalgamation
options.explanation: The standard expressly contemplates adjusting consideration for future events.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT among the factors AS 14 lists for estimating the useful life of goodwill arising on amalgamation?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U2 — goodwill amortisation factors

readLink.label: Read: AS 14 — the standard at a glance →

options.text: The quantum of securities premium on the shares issued as consideration
options.explanation: Premium on the consideration shares says nothing about how long the acquired earning power lasts.

options.text: Service-life expectancies of key individuals or groups of employees
options.explanation: Expressly listed — goodwill often rides on people.

options.text: Legal, regulatory or contractual provisions affecting the useful life
options.explanation: Expressly listed as a limiting factor.

options.text: The foreseeable life of the business or industry
options.explanation: First factor on the standard's list.

questions.stem: In a merger-type amalgamation under AS 14, the transferor's Profit and Loss Account balance is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U2 — balance of profit and loss account

readLink.label: Read: AS 14 — the standard at a glance →

options.text: Extinguished — it loses its identity
options.explanation: Losing identity is the PURCHASE-method fate of the transferor's P&L balance.

options.text: Aggregated with the transferee's P&L balance, or alternatively transferred to General Reserve
options.explanation: Pooling preserves the revenue character — aggregate, or park in General Reserve.

options.text: Always credited to capital reserve
options.explanation: Capital reserve treatment belongs nowhere in the merger route for the P&L balance.

options.text: Held in a separate 'Transferor P&L' account for five years
options.explanation: No such parking account exists in the standard.

questions.stem: Which disclosure is required for ALL amalgamations in the first financial statements after the amalgamation — regardless of method?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U2 — disclosure requirements

readLink.label: Read: AS 14 — the standard at a glance →

options.text: The period of amortisation of goodwill arising on amalgamation
options.explanation: That belongs to the purchase-method additional list.

options.text: The percentage of each company's equity shares exchanged to effect the amalgamation
options.explanation: That is an ADDITIONAL disclosure for the pooling of interests method only.

options.text: Names and general nature of business of the amalgamating companies, the effective date, the method of accounting, and particulars of the sanctioned scheme
options.explanation: That is the common list; share-exchange percentages and goodwill amortisation periods are method-specific extras.

options.text: A pro-forma P&L as if the companies had always been combined
options.explanation: AS 14 asks for no pro-forma restatement.

questions.stem: AS 14 prescribes the accounting for an amalgamation in the books of:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U2 — introduction, books of transferee

readLink.label: Read: AS 14 — the standard at a glance →

options.text: The transferee company — including the treatment of any resultant goodwill or reserves
options.explanation: The standard governs the surviving company's accounting; the transferor dissolves.

options.text: The holding company of the transferee
options.explanation: Group-level accounting is consolidation territory, not AS 14.

options.text: The transferor company
options.explanation: The transferor is dissolved — closing its books is procedure, not AS 14's subject.

options.text: Both companies equally
options.explanation: Only the transferee's books are the standard's subject.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Case: Tungabhadra Textiles (asset grant, both methods)
questions.case: Tungabhadra Textiles Ltd buys a machine for ₹48,00,000 (useful life 6 years, salvage value ₹6,00,000, straight-line depreciation) and receives a government grant of ₹12,00,000 tied to the purchase. The company is weighing both AS 12 presentations: Method I (deduct the grant from the asset's cost) and Method II (treat the grant as deferred income).

questions.stem: Under Method I, the annual depreciation charge is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — Method I depreciation

options.explanation: That is the annual grant release under Method II, not depreciation.
That forgets the ₹6,00,000 salvage value.
That is the Method II charge — the grant has not been deducted from cost.
Depreciable base (48 − 12 − 6) = 30,00,000 over 6 years.

questions.stem: Under Method II, each year's P&L shows:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — Method II deferred income

options.text: Depreciation ₹8,00,000 and grant income ₹2,00,000
options.explanation: The depreciation base is cost less salvage: 42,00,000 over 6 years.

options.text: Depreciation ₹7,00,000 and deferred grant income ₹2,00,000
options.explanation: Asset stays at 48,00,000: depreciation (48 − 6)/6 = 7,00,000; grant releases 12,00,000/6 = 2,00,000.

options.text: Depreciation ₹5,00,000 and no grant income
options.explanation: That is Method I — under deferred income the asset keeps its gross cost.

options.text: Depreciation ₹7,00,000 and no grant income
options.explanation: The deferred grant must reach the P&L systematically over the life.

questions.stem: Under Method I, the machine's book value at the end of year 2 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — Method I carrying amount

options.explanation: That deducts the salvage value from the book value as well — salvage stays in until disposal.
That runs Method II depreciation (7,00,000 × 2) off the NET cost — mixing the methods.
Recorded cost 36,00,000 less two years' depreciation of 5,00,000 each.
That deducts only one year of depreciation.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Case: Penganga Chemicals (refund of an asset grant)
questions.case: Penganga Chemicals Ltd bought a plant for ₹36,00,000 (residual value ₹3,00,000, useful life 4 years, SLM) and received a grant of ₹9,00,000 which it deducted from the plant's cost. At the start of year 3 — after exactly two years' depreciation — conditions are breached and ₹6,00,000 of the grant becomes refundable. The company pays the refund and adds it to the plant's book value.

questions.stem: The plant's book value immediately after recording the refund is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — refund added to book value

options.explanation: Net cost 27,00,000; depreciation (27 − 3)/4 = 6,00,000 × 2 years → 15,00,000; add the ₹6,00,000 refund.
That adds only half the refund; the full ₹6,00,000 goes back into the asset.
That is the original net cost — two years of depreciation have run.
That is the book value BEFORE adding back the refunded grant.

questions.stem: Depreciation for each of the remaining two years is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — prospective depreciation on revised value

options.explanation: That spreads the revised base over the original 4 years instead of the residual life.
Revised base (21,00,000 − 3,00,000) spread prospectively over the 2 years left.
That continues the old charge — the refund raised the depreciable base.
That divides the full 21,00,000 by 2, forgetting the residual value.

questions.stem: Had the company instead recomputed years 1–2 depreciation on the higher base and adjusted the difference in year 3, that treatment would be:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — no retrospective rework on refund

options.text: Proper, if the auditor concurs
options.explanation: Auditor concurrence cannot override the standard's prospective rule.

options.text: Improper — AS 12 requires prospective depreciation over the residual life; closed years are not reworked
options.explanation: Retrospective adjustment of past depreciation violates the standard.

options.text: Proper, provided the adjustment is shown as a prior period item
options.explanation: There is no prior period error — the refund is a current-period event.

options.text: Proper, as it better matches total cost with total use
options.explanation: However intuitive, the standard mandates the prospective route.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Case: Sharavati Hospitals (revenue grant and its refund)
questions.case: Sharavati Hospitals Ltd receives a lump-sum government grant of ₹75,00,000 to meet the salaries of its rural outreach doctors for the next five years, and credits it to a Deferred Grant account, releasing it to the P&L on a straight-line basis. At the start of year 4, the conditions are breached and the ENTIRE grant of ₹75,00,000 becomes refundable.

questions.stem: Grant income recognised in the P&L in each of years 1–3 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — straight-line release of revenue grant

options.explanation: No basis — the spread is 75,00,000 over five years.
Recognising everything in year 1 mismatches five years of salary support.
Straight-line over the five supported years: 75,00,000 ÷ 5.
That spreads over three years — the grant supports five.

questions.stem: The balance in the Deferred Grant account when the refund is triggered is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — unamortised deferred credit

options.explanation: That is the amount already RELEASED to income, not what remains deferred.
That is one year's release, not the remaining balance.
75,00,000 received less 15,00,000 released for each of three years.
Three years of releases have already left the account.

questions.stem: On refunding the full ₹75,00,000, the P&L is debited with:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U1 — refund exceeding deferred credit

options.explanation: The refund first exhausts the ₹30,00,000 deferred credit; the excess — matching the income already recognised — is charged to the P&L as an extraordinary item.
That is the deferred credit — the P&L takes the EXCESS of the refund over it.

options.text: Nil — the whole refund adjusts reserves
options.explanation: Revenue-grant refunds run through deferred credit and then the P&L, never reserves.
The deferred balance absorbs part of the refund before the P&L is touched.

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 14 · Case: Krishna Foods (the standard's text applied)
questions.case: Krishna Foods Ltd (year end 31 March 20X2) obtains sanction for a scheme to amalgamate Cauvery Agro Ltd into itself, to be accounted under the purchase method. The amalgamation is effected on 15 April 20X2 — after the balance sheet date but before Krishna Foods' financial statements are issued. The scheme also promises the transferor's shareholders an additional cash payment linked to 20X2–X3 profits, which at the date of amalgamation is probable and can be reasonably estimated.

readLink.label: Read: AS 14 — the standard at a glance →

questions.stem: In the financial statements for the year ended 31 March 20X2, the amalgamation is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U2 — amalgamation after the balance sheet date

options.text: Incorporated from 1 April 20X1 as the effective date
options.explanation: Nothing is incorporated in the year the scheme merely straddles — the amalgamation belongs to 20X2–X3.

options.text: Neither incorporated nor disclosed
options.explanation: A sanctioned scheme effected before issuance is precisely what AS 4 disclosure exists for.

options.text: Incorporated at 31 March 20X2 values
options.explanation: The balance sheet date precedes the amalgamation — only AS 4 disclosure this year.

options.text: Disclosed in accordance with AS 4 but not incorporated in the figures
options.explanation: Effected after the balance sheet date → disclosure event, not an adjustment; it may also inform going-concern judgements.

questions.stem: The profit-linked additional payment is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U2 — contingent consideration

options.text: Included in the consideration computed at the date of amalgamation
options.explanation: An adjustment that is probable AND reasonably estimable at the date of amalgamation enters consideration immediately.

options.text: Recognised only when the 20X2–X3 profits are finally determined
options.explanation: Recognise-when-determinable applies only where the payment is NOT probable and reasonably estimable at the date.

options.text: Ignored, since consideration cannot be contingent
options.explanation: AS 14 expressly provides for consideration adjustments contingent on future events.

options.text: Charged to the P&L as a period cost when paid
options.explanation: The payment is consideration to shareholders, not an operating expense.

questions.stem: Once effected, which ADDITIONAL disclosure applies because the purchase method is used?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 9 U2 — purchase-method disclosures

options.text: The consideration (including the contingently payable element) and the treatment of the consideration/net-assets difference — including the goodwill amortisation period
options.explanation: The purchase-method extras ride on top of the common list of names, date, method and scheme particulars.

options.text: A five-year pro-forma of combined results
options.explanation: AS 14 requires no pro-forma combined statements.

options.text: Nothing beyond the common disclosures for all amalgamations
options.explanation: The purchase method carries its own additional list.

options.text: The percentage of each company's equity shares exchanged
options.explanation: That is the pooling-of-interests additional disclosure.

questions.stem: When is a government grant recognised under AS 12, and what are the two broad approaches to accounting for grants? (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Definition: assistance by government in cash or kind for past/future compliance with conditions; excludes unvaluable assistance and normal trading transactions
Recognition: reasonable assurance of BOTH condition compliance and receipt; receipt alone not conclusive
Capital approach: grant as shareholders' funds — for grants in the nature of promoters' contribution
Income approach: grant to income over one or more periods, matched with related costs — for other grants

questions.stem: Describe the two acceptable presentations of a government grant related to a specific fixed asset, including the treatment where the grant equals the whole cost and where the asset is non-depreciable. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Method I: deduct from gross value; P&L benefit via reduced depreciation; grant = whole/virtually whole cost → asset at nominal value
Method II: deferred income released to P&L on a systematic and rational basis over the useful life (in depreciation proportions)
Non-depreciable asset grants → capital reserve; if obligations attached → income over the obligation-cost period

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 12 · Revenue and promoters' grants
questions.stem: How are (a) grants related to revenue, (b) grants compensating past expenses/losses, and (c) grants in the nature of promoters' contribution presented? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Revenue grants: credit in P&L (separately or Other Income) or deduction from the related expense; multi-year support spread straight-line
Past-cost compensation / immediate support with no further costs: P&L of the period receivable, extraordinary item if appropriate (AS 5)
Promoters' contribution: capital reserve — neither dividend nor deferred income

questions.stem: State the treatment when a government grant becomes refundable, for each kind of grant. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Character: refund is an extraordinary item (AS 5)
Revenue grant: first against unamortised deferred credit; excess (or whole, if none) immediately to P&L
Asset grant: increase book value OR reduce deferred income; where book value increased, depreciate revised amount PROSPECTIVELY over residual life
Promoters' contribution: reduce capital reserve by the amount recoverable

questions.topic: Ch 9 · AS 14 · Scope and post-year-end schemes
questions.stem: What does AS 14 apply to, what is expressly outside it, and how is an amalgamation effected after the balance sheet date dealt with? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Applies to amalgamations under the Companies Act 2013 / other statutes, in the TRANSFEREE company's books, incl. resultant goodwill/reserves
Acquisitions excluded: acquired company not dissolved, separate entity continues
After balance sheet date but before issuance: AS 4 disclosure, not incorporated; may still bear on going concern

questions.stem: How is consideration measured under AS 14 where future adjustments are possible, and how is goodwill arising on amalgamation treated? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Consideration: aggregate of shares/securities/cash/other assets to the transferor's SHAREHOLDERS, elements at fair value
Future-event adjustment: include at amalgamation date if probable and reasonably estimable; else recognise when determinable
Goodwill: amortise ≤5 years unless longer justified; factors — business/industry life, obsolescence and demand, key-personnel service lives, competitor actions, legal/contractual limits

questions.stem: List the disclosures required in the first financial statements following an amalgamation — common, pooling-specific and purchase-specific. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: All amalgamations: names and general nature of business; effective date for accounting; method used; particulars of the sanctioned scheme
Pooling extras: description and number of shares issued + percentage of each company's equity exchanged; consideration/net-assets difference and treatment
Purchase extras: consideration incl. contingently payable part; difference and treatment incl. goodwill amortisation period
